topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy falling onto a desk. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly before I finally clicked it open. The words swam before my eyes, a series of polite, impersonal refusals. A scholarship. The one thing that felt like the foundation of my entire future, now reduced to a cold, hard rejection. I reread the rejection letter three times, each time the same sense of disconnect washed over me. My stomach tightened, a knot forming just beneath my ribs. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and stared blankly at the wall, tracing the pattern of the wallpaper with my eyes, searching for some logic in the nonsensical outcome. Why? I felt a sudden, sharp dryness in my throat, making it difficult to swallow.

[story 2]

I kept running my thumb over the screen, a repetitive, frantic motion. The screen reflected back my own face—pale, eyes wide, mouth slightly downturned. This couldn't be right. I reread the rejection notice, my breath catching in shallow gasps. The entire structure of my plans, the late nights studying, the sacrifices I’d made—it all seemed to have dissolved into this single, damning piece of correspondence. I pushed my chair back abruptly, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding deafening in the quiet dorm room. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the sprawling, indifferent campus below. The sunlight felt harsh, illuminating the sheer emptiness of the situation. I let out a slow, shaky exhale, the air tasting thin and metallic.

[story 3]

"Denied." The single word echoed in the silence of my small apartment. I picked up the printed notification, the crisp paper feeling alien and flimsy in my hand. I didn't move to throw it away; instead, I smoothed the edges meticulously, as if that could somehow alter the printed truth. My hands began to shake visibly, a fine tremor running up my forearms. I paced the length of the living room three times, stopping abruptly by the bookshelf. Every book spine seemed to mock me with its permanence, its established knowledge, while my own trajectory had just been violently derailed. A profound sense of disorientation settled over me, making the room feel suddenly too large, too empty. I just stood there, hands clenched at my sides, unable to summon the energy to even look at the door.
